title: How to Evict a Tenant in New Jersey: Process, Notices, and Timeline
titleTag: How to Evict a Tenant in New Jersey (2026)
description: How to evict a tenant in New Jersey: good-cause grounds, required notices, Special Civil Part filing, the warrant of removal, and realistic timelines.
author: Proactive Property Management
body:
New Jersey is one of the most tenant-protective states in the country, and eviction here is strictly a court process. The Anti-Eviction Act lists the only legal grounds for removing a tenant, and every step — notice, filing, hearing, lockout — runs on statutory rails. Serve the wrong notice or skip a required step, and the court dismisses the case and you start over.

This guide walks small landlords through the full sequence: the good-cause grounds, the notices each ground requires, filing in the Special Civil Part, the hearing, the warrant of removal, and how long it all realistically takes.

Good cause under the Anti-Eviction Act

For most residential rentals, N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.1 controls. A lease expiring is not, by itself, a reason to remove a tenant — you need one of the statute's roughly 18 enumerated grounds. The ones small landlords rely on most:

  • Nonpayment of rent
  • Habitual late payment of rent, after written notice
  • Continued violation of lease rules or covenants, after written notice
  • Disorderly conduct that disturbs the peace of other residents
  • Willful or grossly negligent damage to the property
  • The owner of a building with three or fewer residential units intends to personally occupy the unit, or has contracted to sell it to a buyer who will

One meaningful exception: owner-occupied buildings with two or fewer rental units generally fall outside the Act, so those landlords can end a month-to-month tenancy with one full month's notice without proving good cause. Confirm how your building is classified first.

Notice requirements depend on the ground

Each ground carries its own notice sequence, and getting it wrong is the most common reason cases are dismissed.

GroundNotice to ceaseNotice to quit
Nonpayment of rentNot requiredGenerally not required — file directly
Habitual late paymentRequiredOne month
Lease violationsRequiredOne month
Disorderly conductRequired3 days
Willful damageNot required3 days
Owner move-in (3 or fewer units)Not required2 months

Nonpayment is the outlier: you can generally file in court once rent goes unpaid, with no notice to quit — though subsidized housing programs and some leases add their own notice steps, so check yours. Conduct grounds run in two stages: a notice to cease comes first and gives the tenant a chance to correct the behavior; a notice to quit ends the tenancy if the behavior continues. Serve every notice in writing, keep proof of service, and confirm the current period for your ground — statutory periods run from three days to three years.

Filing the summary dispossess action

Eviction cases — formally, summary dispossess actions — are filed in the Superior Court, Special Civil Part, Landlord/Tenant Section in the county where the property sits. Two prerequisites catch small landlords:

  • Registration. Your rental must have a valid landlord registration on file — with the municipal clerk, or the DCA for buildings of three or more units. Courts will not enter judgment for an unregistered landlord. See our guide to NJ landlord obligations.
  • Entity ownership. If the property is owned by an LLC or corporation, New Jersey requires a licensed attorney to represent it in court — the owner cannot appear personally on the entity's behalf.

After filing, the court schedules a hearing, commonly 2 to 5 weeks out depending on county backlog.

The hearing and judgment for possession

Most counties send cases to a settlement conference first, and many resolve there with a payment plan or an agreed move-out date. Cases that do not settle are tried to a judge, without a jury. The landlord must prove the ground, the notices, and the amounts claimed. In a nonpayment case, a tenant who pays everything owed on or before the trial date generally gets the case dismissed.

If the landlord prevails, the court enters a judgment for possession — meaning you are legally entitled to the unit back. It does not, by itself, remove anyone.

The warrant of removal

A warrant of removal may be issued no earlier than 3 business days after the judgment for possession. It is executed by a Special Civil Part court officer — not by the landlord or police you arrange. After the officer serves the warrant, the tenant generally has 3 more business days before a lockout can occur. Tenants can also ask the court for extra time: an orderly removal (commonly up to 7 days) or, in genuine hardship cases where the rent is paid, a hardship stay of up to 6 months. Those are the court's decisions, not the landlord's.

Illegal lockouts and self-help

Changing the locks, removing doors, shutting off utilities, or putting a tenant's belongings out without a court officer is an illegal lockout under New Jersey law — a disorderly persons offense. The tenant can be restored to possession by court order and can sue for damages. The only lawful removal is a court officer executing a warrant.

Realistic timelines — and what resets the clock

An uncontested nonpayment case commonly runs about 6 weeks to 2.5 months from filing to lockout. Contested cases, adjournments, and post-judgment stays push that to 3 months or more, and conduct grounds add their notice periods before you can even file.

Procedural missteps reset the clock because dismissals are usually without prejudice: you may refile, but from the beginning — including re-serving notices. Common resets: a short or vague notice, accepting rent after serving a notice to quit on a conduct ground, filing while unregistered, and naming the wrong owner on the complaint. On a conduct case with a one-month notice, one bad notice costs you two months. Many owners hand this process to an attorney or a property manager for exactly that reason.

Frequently asked questions

Can I evict a tenant in New Jersey without going to court?

No. Every residential eviction in New Jersey requires a judgment for possession and a warrant of removal executed by a court officer. Self-help removal is illegal regardless of what the lease says or how much rent is owed.

How long does an eviction take in New Jersey?

Commonly 6 weeks to 3 months from filing for a straightforward nonpayment case, and longer when the case is contested, the tenant obtains a stay, or the ground requires pre-filing notices. County backlogs vary, so treat any estimate as a range.

Can I apply the security deposit to unpaid rent during an eviction?

Not unilaterally while the tenancy continues. After the tenant vacates, the deposit is handled under New Jersey's deposit statute, with an itemized accounting due on a 30-day deadline — see our security deposit guide for the rules.
